refined_fr.refined_fr: CR-NGHE-003 Refined: The NGHE solution shall enable operators and tele-operators to achieve a measured energy efficiency of energy_per_ton ≤ E_target (customer-defined threshold) over the agreed evaluation period, through integrated monitoring, control optimization, and real-time feedback mechanisms.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR is outcome-focused but lacks explicit linkage to the mechanisms (monitoring, control, feedback) that enable efficiency achievement. Refinement clarifies that energy efficiency is not a passive property but an active capability requiring measurement, visibility, and operator decision support. This aligns with the high safety criticality by ensuring operators have visibility into energy consumption patterns.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure energy_per_ton = (total_energy_consumed_kWh / total_tonnage_moved) across a defined evaluation period (e.g., 40-hour work week). Verify that measured value ≤ E_target. Collect telemetry from Vehicle power systems and cross-validate with site energy meters. Confirm operator feedback mechanisms are functional and used.
sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh) and work output (tonnage moved) at the Vehicle level.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit energy and productivity telemetry to the operator (local or remote) in real-time via Telemetry interface.
CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall provide operator-accessible feedback (dashboard, alerts, recommendations) on energy efficiency status relative to E_target.
CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall support tele-operator control modes that enable remote energy-efficient operation without compromising safety.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Implement energy metering at the Vehicle power subsystem; aggregate energy and productivity data in a central telemetry service; expose real-time KPI dashboard to operator interfaces (local HMI and remote tele-operator console).

DP-B: Embed energy efficiency optimization algorithms in the Vehicle control stack; use predictive models to recommend efficient operating modes; integrate feedback loop between operator commands and energy consumption feedback.

DP-C: Distribute energy monitoring across Vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, drivetrain); aggregate via a dedicated Energy Management Module; expose KPI via Telemetry and SafetyAlert interfaces; couple with operator training and worksite energy policies.

DP-D: Implement a hybrid approach: Vehicle-level energy metering + centralized telemetry aggregation + operator feedback dashboard + tele-operator control optimization + integration with Safety Services for energy-related safety alerts (e.g., overload warnings).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-D: Hybrid integrated energy efficiency architecture combining Vehicle-level metering, centralized telemetry aggregation, operator feedback dashboard, tele-operator control optimization, and Safety Services integration for energy-related alerts.

potential_sources: Vehicle power subsystem design must expose energy consumption data via standardized interface (low coupling risk if interface is well-defined).
Telemetry service must aggregate data from Vehicle without imposing tight timing constraints (manageable via asynchronous messaging).
Operator feedback dashboard must respond to real-time telemetry without blocking Vehicle control loops (decoupled via event-driven architecture).
Tele-operator control optimization must not introduce latency that compromises safety (mitigated by Safety Services validation).
coupling_check.notes: DP-D exhibits acceptable coupling because each functional element (metering, aggregation, feedback, optimization, safety integration) can be designed with clear interfaces. The primary coupling risk is between Vehicle control and tele-operator optimization; this is managed by Safety Services as a gating mechanism.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Vehicle power subsystem can expose energy consumption data with sufficient granularity (kWh-level accuracy) and frequency (≥1 Hz sampling).
ASSUMPTION: Telemetry network (Network Link) has sufficient bandwidth and latency characteristics to support real-time energy data streaming without impacting Vehicle control responsiveness.
ASSUMPTION: Operator (local and remote) has cognitive capacity to interpret energy efficiency feedback and adjust behavior accordingly.
RISK: If Vehicle metering hardware is not available or is cost-prohibitive, energy estimation models may be required, introducing accuracy uncertainty.
RISK: If tele-operator control optimization introduces latency >500ms, safety-critical operations may be compromised.
RISK: If Safety Services does not validate energy-related alerts (e.g., overload warnings), operator may receive conflicting guidance.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Clarify the interface contract between Vehicle power subsystem and Telemetry service (data format, sampling rate, accuracy tolerance). Define tele-operator control optimization as a non-safety-critical advisory layer, with Safety Services as the authoritative safety gating mechanism. Establish energy efficiency target (E_target) as a configurable parameter, not a hard-coded constant.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: The NGHE Solution module is the top-level system boundary that orchestrates energy efficiency capability across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services. It owns the integration logic for energy metering aggregation, telemetry routing, operator feedback synthesis, and safety-critical energy alert validation.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Bidirectional interface for energy consumption data (kWh, tonnage moved, efficiency KPI) flowing from Vehicle to operator dashboards, and operator feedback/commands flowing back to Vehicle control.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Operator task commands (work assignments, energy efficiency targets, operational modes) flow into NGHE Solution to configure energy optimization behavior.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Energy-related safety alerts (overload warnings, efficiency threshold violations, tele-operator latency warnings) flow from NGHE Solution to Safety Services and operator interfaces.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network transport for Telemetry, Tasking, and SafetyAlert interfaces; ensures connectivity between Vehicle, Teleoperation, and operator interfaces.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the natural top-level container for energy efficiency orchestration. It owns the integration logic, telemetry aggregation, and operator feedback synthesis. Minimal diffusion: only Vehicle-level metering hardware is external.

Vehicle module owns energy metering hardware and low-level power subsystem monitoring. However, energy efficiency KPI computation, telemetry aggregation, and operator feedback are better owned by NGHE Solution to avoid tight coupling between Vehicle control and operator interfaces.

Teleoperation module owns tele-operator control optimization and remote command execution. However, energy efficiency monitoring and feedback synthesis are better owned by NGHE Solution to ensure consistency across local and remote operators.

Safety Services owns energy-related safety alert validation and operator safety guidance. However, energy efficiency KPI computation and feedback are better owned by NGHE Solution to maintain separation of concerns (efficiency vs. safety).

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the optimal container because it sits at the system integration layer, with visibility into Vehicle telemetry, operator interfaces (local and remote), and Safety Services. This placement enables energy efficiency to be treated as a system-level capability rather than a Vehicle-only or Teleoperation-only concern. The module owns the aggregation logic, KPI computation, and feedback synthesis, while delegating metering hardware to Vehicle and safety validation to Safety Services.
containment_risks: RISK: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic integration hub, it may accumulate too many responsibilities (energy, safety, telemetry, tasking). Mitigation: Define clear sub-module boundaries within NGHE Solution (e.g., Energy Efficiency Manager, Telemetry Aggregator, Feedback Synthesizer).

RISK: If Vehicle metering hardware is not standardized, NGHE Solution may need to implement multiple adapter patterns, increasing complexity. Mitigation: Establish a Vehicle telemetry interface contract early in design.

RISK: If Teleoperation module does not expose energy-aware control APIs, tele-operator optimization may be difficult to implement. Mitigation: Ensure Teleoperation module provides hooks for energy-efficient command execution.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Define NGHE Solution as a system orchestrator with explicit sub-module responsibilities: (1) Energy Efficiency Manager (owns KPI computation and feedback logic), (2) Telemetry Aggregator (owns data collection and routing), (3) Operator Feedback Synthesizer (owns dashboard and alert generation). Establish clear interface contracts between NGHE Solution and Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services to minimize coupling.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-D (hybrid integrated architecture) directly addresses all sub-FRs derived from CR-NGHE-003 Refined. The DP specifies mechanisms for energy metering (FR-003.1), telemetry transmission (FR-003.2), operator feedback (FR-003.3), and tele-operator control optimization (FR-003.4). The DP is implementable and does not introduce infeasible assumptions.

diffusion_risks: Energy metering hardware is distributed across Vehicle subsystems (engine, hydraulics, drivetrain), but aggregation logic is centralized in NGHE Solution. This is acceptable because the interface between Vehicle and NGHE Solution is well-defined (Telemetry).

Tele-operator control optimization logic may be split between Teleoperation module (command execution) and NGHE Solution (energy feedback). Mitigation: Establish clear ownership boundaries and ensure Teleoperation module exposes energy-aware APIs.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is the appropriate container for energy efficiency orchestration because it owns the system-level integration logic. Vehicle owns metering hardware, Teleoperation owns remote command execution, and Safety Services owns safety validation. This distribution minimizes coupling and aligns with the system architecture.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: CR-NGHE-003 Refined can be verified by measuring energy_per_ton over the agreed evaluation period. Verification requires: (1) Vehicle metering hardware to be functional and calibrated, (2) Telemetry interface to reliably transmit energy data, (3) Operator feedback mechanisms to be accessible and usable. All three are achievable within NGHE Solution ownership.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns energy efficiency orchestration, Vehicle owns metering hardware, Teleoperation owns remote control optimization, and Safety Services owns safety validation. Each module has a distinct responsibility, and interfaces are well-defined.

recommended_revisions: Establish a detailed interface contract between Vehicle and NGHE Solution for energy telemetry (data format, sampling rate, accuracy tolerance).
Define tele-operator control optimization as a non-safety-critical advisory layer, with explicit safety gating by Safety Services.
Specify energy efficiency target (E_target) as a configurable parameter in Tasking interface, not a hard-coded constant.
Establish acceptance criteria for operator feedback mechanisms (e.g., dashboard response time <1 second, alert delivery <5 seconds).

decision.justification: CR-NGHE-003 Refined is not atomic; it decomposes into four sub-FRs (energy metering, telemetry transmission, operator feedback, tele-operator optimization). Each sub-FR requires further refinement and DP selection to ensure implementability and safety compliance. The high safety criticality factor necessitates detailed analysis of tele-operator control optimization and Safety Services integration before proceeding to detailed design.
next_level_sub_frs: CR-NGHE-003.1: The NGHE solution shall continuously measure and log energy consumption (kWh) and work output (tonnage moved) at the Vehicle level with ≥1 Hz sampling frequency and ±5% accuracy tolerance.
CR-NGHE-003.2: The NGHE solution shall transmit energy and productivity telemetry to the operator (local or remote) in real-time via Telemetry interface with <500ms latency and 99.5% reliability.

CR-NGHE-003.3: The NGHE solution shall provide operator-accessible feedback (dashboard, alerts, recommendations) on energy efficiency status relative to E_target, with dashboard response time <1 second and alert delivery <5 seconds.

CR-NGHE-003.4: The NGHE solution shall support tele-operator control modes that enable remote energy-efficient operation without compromising safety, with Safety Services validation of all energy-related control commands.

assumptions: Vehicle power subsystem can expose energy consumption data with ≥1 Hz sampling frequency and ±5% accuracy.
Telemetry network (Network Link) has sufficient bandwidth and latency characteristics (<500ms) to support real-time energy data streaming.
Operator (local and remote) has cognitive capacity to interpret energy efficiency feedback and adjust behavior accordingly.
Safety Services can validate energy-related alerts and control commands without introducing unacceptable latency.
Energy efficiency target (E_target) is customer-defined and configurable via Tasking interface.
open_risks: Vehicle metering hardware may not be available or cost-prohibitive; energy estimation models may be required, introducing accuracy uncertainty.
Tele-operator control optimization may introduce latency >500ms, compromising safety-critical operations.
Operator feedback mechanisms may not be intuitive or accessible, reducing effectiveness of energy efficiency improvements.
Safety Services integration may introduce conflicts between energy efficiency optimization and safety constraints.
Energy efficiency target (E_target) may be unrealistic or unachievable with current Vehicle design, requiring design iteration.
evidence_needed: Vehicle power subsystem specification (metering hardware, data interfaces, sampling rates, accuracy).
Telemetry network performance characteristics (bandwidth, latency, reliability).
Operator interface design (dashboard mockups, alert formats, feedback mechanisms).
Safety Services interface contract (energy-related alert types, validation logic, command gating).
Customer energy efficiency target (E_target value, evaluation period, acceptance criteria).
Tele-operator control optimization algorithm specification (latency budget, safety constraints, optimization objectives).
